title: The new politics of power | Wood Mackenzie
gist: US residential electricity prices have surged over 40% since 2021, driven by a utility business model that structurally rewards capital spending; investor-owned utilities are planning $1.4 trillion in capex over five years, while solutions including better grid utilisation, business model reform, and improved load forecasting are emerging.

themes.theme: US Electricity Price Surge and Political Backlash

insights.claim: US residential electricity prices have risen by more than 40% since the start of 2021, outpacing general inflation significantly.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Affordability pressure at scale creates regulatory and political risk for investor-owned utilities, potentially accelerating rate case scrutiny and constraining future capex recovery.
insights.quote: US residential electricity prices have risen by more than 40 per cent since the start of 2021, which is much faster than general inflation.

insights.claim: Utilities requested a total of $31 billion in increased rates in 2025, double the amount requested in 2024, signalling an accelerating pace of rate-case filings.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A doubling of rate requests in a single year is a leading indicator of consumer bill shock — investors in regulated utilities must weigh regulatory pushback risk against capex recovery.
insights.quote: Utilities requested a total of $31 billion in increased rates last year, double the amount in 2024.

insights.claim: Investor-owned utilities are planning to spend $1.4 trillion on capital projects over the next five years — equivalent on one calculation to building almost 2,000 Hoover Dams at today's prices.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: The $1.4 trillion capex pipeline is a massive equity story for utility suppliers, EPC contractors, and equipment manufacturers — but also the root cause of ongoing bill increases that could trigger regulatory constraint.
insights.quote: investor-owned utilities are planning to spend $1.4 trillion on capital projects over the next five years – enough on one calculation, to build almost 2,000 Hoover Dams at today's prices.

insights.claim: A Powerlines-commissioned Ipsos poll found that four in five Americans feel powerless about energy costs, and the share who believe state officials serve their interests as consumers fell from 38% to 29% in a single year.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Rapid erosion of consumer trust in energy regulators creates political demand for intervention, raising the risk of populist rate caps or forced business model changes that could impair utility returns.
insights.quote: four in five Americans feel powerless about energy costs. The proportion who believe their state officials are serving their interests as consumers fell from 38 per cent to 29 per cent in a single year.

insights.claim: The rising cost of electricity has moved from a niche regulatory domain to mainstream political attention, reaching governors' offices and the White House — a development characterised as 'a new politics of electricity'.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Politicisation of electricity pricing increases the probability of legislated rate relief measures, which could disrupt the utility capex-recovery model underpinning the $1.4 trillion investment plan.
insights.quote: Charles calls this "a new politics of electricity." It is a domain that until recently sat outside mainstream political attention, but now reaches governors' offices and the White House.

themes.theme: Root Causes of Electricity Cost Inflation

insights.claim: The top five cost drivers of rising US electricity bills identified by the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory are: fuel and wholesale supply, distribution costs, generation capex, transmission costs, and cost recovery from extreme weather events.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Investors analysing utility rate cases should weight these five categories when modelling revenue requirements — none is disappearing near-term, making sustained bill inflation structurally likely.

insights.claim: The traditional utility business model structurally rewards capital spending, making it a fundamental driver of rising bills beyond any specific line-item cost.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Without business model reform, even aggressive grid efficiency gains will be partially offset by incentive-driven capex; investors should monitor regulatory innovations that restructure utility return frameworks.
insights.quote: Charles points beyond the line items to a fundamental issue: the traditional utility business model, which structurally rewards capital spending.

insights.claim: Data centres have not been a meaningful driver of electricity price increases across most of the US until now, but PJM capacity auction prices have rocketed, providing an early signal that the picture is starting to change.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: PJM capacity price spikes are a leading indicator of where data-centre load growth will begin showing up in consumer bills — utilities and regulators in PJM territory face the earliest reckoning.
insights.quote: PJM's capacity auction, where prices have rocketed, is one early signal that the picture is starting to change.

insights.claim: The US electricity grid is currently running at roughly 50% utilisation, and extracting more from existing infrastructure is identified as the first and fastest solution to rising bills.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Grid utilisation technologies that improve throughput at low capital cost represent a large opportunity — companies offering 'software for the grid' or advanced conductors stand to benefit as regulators push efficiency over new build.
insights.quote: get more out of the existing grid, which is currently running at roughly 50 per cent utilisation, through technologies he describes as "ibuprofen for the grid."

insights.claim: Modernising the utility business model along the lines of the UK's totex approach — where utilities can earn a return on operational as well as capital spending — is proposed as a structural fix for perverse capex incentives.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: If US states adopt totex-style regulation, the competitive dynamics for utility suppliers shift significantly from pure capex supply toward managed service and operational contracts.
insights.quote: potentially drawing on the UK's totex approach, where utilities can earn a return on operational as well as capital spending.

insights.claim: Improved grid planning — particularly better load forecasting and more rigorous integrated resource planning — is identified as a third structural solution to electricity cost inflation.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Companies providing demand forecasting analytics and grid planning software to utilities are positioned to capture regulatory mandated investment in planning capacity upgrades.
insights.quote: improve grid planning, particularly how load is forecast and how integrated resource plans are built.

insights.claim: Public Utilities Commissions — approximately 200 commissioners overseeing roughly $200 billion in annual spending — are the critical but overlooked decision-makers determining what consumers ultimately pay for electricity.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Regulatory advocacy and commissioner engagement is becoming a material investment risk factor — companies that track and influence PUC proceedings have an information advantage.
insights.quote: the roughly 200 commissioners across the country who oversee around $200 billion in annual spending and ultimately determine what consumers pay.
